description._content: visit the screenpunk slide show at www.screenjunkies.nl

According to Mark Fisher, it is easier to imagine the end of the world than the end of capitalism. According to Karl Marx, that end is inevitably embedded in the course of human history. According to Yanis Varoufakis — visionary and the finance minister who tried to steer Greece through the debt crisis in 2015 — the end of capitalism has, in fact, already taken place. And honestly, I think that if the Strait of Hormuz were to remain closed for another year, almost every form of economic activity would grind to a halt on its own.

What actually happens to a society when capitalism ceases to exist? And how do people respond?

In the series What Do You Do When The Walls Come Tumbling Down?, I imagine — from a post-authentic expressionist perspective — possible reactions to the end of capitalism. The question, of course, remains: what do you do when everything falls apart?

description._content: I read Coetzee's The Master of Petersburg about Fjodor Dostoevsky returning to St. Petersburg, after his stepson died. I used ChatGPT to elaborate on it's themes and rendered some of it outcomes in Midjourney. More on aiart-processing on screenpunk.nl

"Rorschach Psychodiagnostics refers to the use of the Rorschach inkblot test to assess an individual's personality, emotional functioning, and thought processes through their interpretations of ambiguous inkblot images, as described in Hermann Rorschach's seminal 1921 book Psychodiagnostics. The test, developed from empirical research, aims to reveal aspects of the personality that are projected onto these visual stimuli, providing psychodiagnostic information to understand mental health and psychological conditions. "

Monomedia is an enquiry into the history of pschychiatry by Fiona Tan. The Screenpunk series contains surrealist extensions of thoughts, themes and texts from the enquiry. Exhibition in Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam, 4 July - 14 September. Book: ISBN 978-94-6208-907-5

Forest-Coded Dispositions

This series explores how perception and posture are shaped by environment. Inspired by the tarsier and Bourdieu’s concept of habitus, the works visualize a body formed by the logic of the forest: vertical space, silence, and readiness.

Through layered textures, anatomical motifs, and symbolic forms, the images reflect how beings embody the structures they live within. It is not just adaptation, but a way of inhabiting the world—a patterned disposition made visible.

title._content: Obducted Analyzed Transformed and Returned by Aliens

description._content: Experimenting with new MJ insights the other day, I was listening to Kendra Morris. To see what would happen I inserted her name in the prompt in combination with various album names. Whatever happened, for one, Kendra Morris is not an unified entity in the MJ libraries. Banshee

"Selkie" refers to a creature from Scottish and Irish folklore. It's a shapeshifter that can transform between a human and a seal. Selkies are often depicted as being able to shed their seal skin to become human, and vice versa, spending time in both worlds.

In Japanese folklore, kitsune (狐, きつね) refers to foxes, particularly those possessing supernatural abilities, including shapeshifting and trickery. They are a type of yōkai, supernatural beings, often associated with the Shinto deity Inari. Kitsune can be benevolent, serving as messengers of Inari, or mischievous and even malevolent tricksters.

title._content: On a Luxurious Berlin Hotel Zimmer

description._content: A phrenological head is a model of the human head used in the now-discredited practice of phrenology, which proposed that a person's character and mental abilities could be determined by examining the shape and bumps on their skull. Phrenologists believed that different areas of the brain were responsible for specific mental faculties, and that the size of these areas could be inferred from the external contours of the skull.

The King The Advisor And The Jester

description._content: Djinn, also spelled jinn, are supernatural creatures from Islamic and pre-Islamic Arabian mythology, often referred to as genies in English. They are beings with free will, capable of both good and evil, and are believed to inhabit a realm parallel to the human world. Djinn are said to be created from smokeless fire, unlike humans who are made from clay or dirt, or angels who are made from light.

description._content: Building an AI-series inspired by Monica Rotgans' van kleur naar kunst (How Colour Turns to Art)

"False Depth of Field" is a qualification that Ahab Abdel-Aziz used to describe one of my images. I took his description and restructured it as a prompt to achieve output in our project "Infinite Layering".
Attempts to express the imaginary image property "infinite layers" over a foreground, and a background layer.
This series involves elements and subjects from the colonial West Indies and Amazon area. The images apply the style of German/ Dutch botanical artist Maria Sibylla Merian (1647 - 1717)

Memorizing Bob Ross, Happy Accidents can be considered artificial generated images that are so far beyond intention that you have to twist your brain to realize that you (or the machine? who was it?) created something extraordinary.
Few times per week I travel by train. Some casual encounters with other passengers I reconstruct over AI.
